title: Pfizer — Vaccines — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: Pfizer Vaccines Portfolio (Abrysvo, Prevnar 20, Penbraya)

Portfolio: Abrysvo (RSV), Prevnar 20 (PCV20, pneumococcal), Penbraya (MenABCWY, meningococcal) Manufacturer: Pfizer Inc. Document scope: Vaccine landscape, product profiles, competitive dynamics, ACIP/access dynamics Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscapes

1.1 Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) — Abrysvo

RSV is a common respiratory virus that typically causes mild, cold-like symptoms in healthy adults but can lead to severe lower respiratory tract disease (LRTD), hospitalization, and death in high-risk populations:

  • Older adults (60+): RSV causes ~60,000-120,000 hospitalizations and 6,000-10,000 deaths annually in the US among adults 65+. Those with COPD, CHF, diabetes, or immunocompromised states face the highest risk
  • Maternal/infant setting: RSV is the leading cause of infant hospitalization in the US (~58,000-80,000 hospitalizations/year in children <5). Seasonal peaks occur October-March in most of the continental US
  • Seasonality: Critical market dynamic — RSV vaccines are seasonal products, with the vaccination window running roughly August-January for the upcoming RSV season

Key market segmentation:

  • Older adults (75+): Universal CDC recommendation since June 2024
  • Adults 50-74 with risk factors: Shared clinical decision-making recommended
  • Maternal immunization: Abrysvo is the only RSV vaccine approved for pregnant women (32-36 weeks gestation, September-January)

1.2 Pneumococcal Disease — Prevnar 20

Streptococcus pneumoniae causes invasive pneumococcal disease (IPD) including bacteremia, meningitis, and pneumococcal pneumonia. It remains a leading cause of vaccine-preventable death in the US:

  • Adults 50+: The primary market for adult pneumococcal conjugate vaccines. In October 2024, ACIP expanded the recommendation from 65+ to include all adults 50+ who are PCV-naive — this is the pivotal market expansion event for Prevnar 20
  • Adults 19-49 with risk conditions: Risk-based recommendation unchanged (diabetes, chronic heart/lung/liver disease, smoking, alcoholism, immunocompromise)
  • Pediatric: Prevnar 20 is also approved for children, though the pediatric market has been long-established with Prevnar 13

Addressable market expansion: The October 2024 ACIP vote to recommend PCV for all adults 50+ (previously 65+) added approximately 30 million vaccine-eligible adults in the US, representing the largest single market expansion event for the pneumococcal franchise in a decade.

1.3 Meningococcal Disease — Penbraya

Meningococcal disease, caused by Neisseria meningitidis, is rare but devastating — case fatality rates of 10-15% and severe sequelae (limb loss, neurological damage) in 10-20% of survivors. Five serogroups (A, B, C, W, Y) cause the majority of disease:

  • Adolescents (11-25 years): Primary target population. Before Penbraya, vaccination against all five serogroups required two separate vaccine series — MenACWY (routine at 11-12, booster at 16) and MenB (shared clinical decision, 16-23)
  • Epidemiology: ~350 meningococcal cases annually in the US; serogroup B accounts for ~35-40% of adolescent/young adult cases
  • College-age risk: Outbreaks on college campuses drive awareness and demand spikes

2. Product Profiles

2.1 Abrysvo (RSV Vaccine)

Attribute Detail
Approval May 2023 (adults 60+); August 2023 (maternal, 32-36 weeks gestation)
Mechanism Bivalent prefusion F protein subunit vaccine (RSV A + RSV B)
Dosing Single dose, intramuscular
Indications Prevention of RSV-associated LRTD in adults 60+; prevention of RSV-associated LRTD in infants from birth through 6 months via maternal immunization
Label expansion FDA approved for adults 18-59 at increased risk (2024)
FY 2025 Revenue ~$1 billion globally; Q4 2025 showed 136% operational growth ($270M increase) driven by international launch uptake
CDC Recommendation Adults 75+ (universal); adults 50-74 at increased risk (shared decision). Maternal: weeks 32-36 gestation, September-January

Key clinical data:

  • RENOIR trial: 66.7% efficacy against RSV-associated LRTD with >=2 symptoms in adults 60+; 78.8% efficacy against severe RSV LRTD
  • Maternal trial: 81.8% efficacy against severe RSV LRTD in infants within first 90 days of life; 69.4% within first 180 days

Challenges:

  • CDC narrowed the older adult recommendation in 2024 — from all adults 60+ (shared decision) to universal for 75+ only, with 50-74 requiring risk factors. This reduced the target population and created confusion among HCPs
  • Maternal market impacted by competition from nirsevimab (Beyfortus, Sanofi/AZ) monoclonal antibody for direct infant immunization — many physicians prefer the passive immunity approach
  • Seasonal revenue concentration creates quarter-to-quarter volatility

2.2 Prevnar 20 (PCV20, Pneumococcal 20-valent Conjugate Vaccine)

Attribute Detail
Approval June 2021 (adults 18+); April 2023 (pediatric, 6 weeks through 17 years)
Mechanism 20-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine covering serotypes 1, 3, 4, 5, 6A, 6B, 7F, 8, 9V, 10A, 11A, 12F, 14, 15B, 18C, 19A, 19F, 22F, 23F, 33F
Dosing Single dose for adults (one and done); series for pediatric
FY 2025 Revenue Prevnar family grew 8% operationally in Q4 2025; Pfizer reached 3 billionth dose milestone. Prevnar family remains ~$6B+ annual franchise
CDC/ACIP October 2024: Single dose PCV recommended for all adults 50+ (expanded from 65+). Risk-based for ages 19-49. Single-dose regimen positions Prevnar 20 favorably vs. the older PCV15+PPSV23 sequential regimen

Competitive threat from Capvaxive (PCV21, Merck):

  • FDA approved June 2024 for adults 18+
  • Covers 21 serotypes; claims 84% coverage of IPD in adults 50+ vs. 52% for Prevnar 20's serotypes
  • Key differentiator: Capvaxive includes 8 unique serotypes not in Prevnar 20 (targeting serotypes responsible for residual disease burden among vaccinated adults)
  • However, Capvaxive does NOT cover serotype 4, which is prevalent in the US and IS covered by Prevnar 20
  • "Serotype counting" anchor emerging in HCP conversations: some physicians and patients equate higher serotype count with superior protection, regardless of epidemiologic relevance

ZoomRx HCP-Patient conversation insights (from studies, n=20):

  • Prevnar 20 holds strongest HCP mind-share: recommended in 12/20 conversations explicitly by name
  • "One and done" messaging is the single most effective acceptance driver — patients respond strongly to simplicity over technical efficacy data
  • 85% overall vaccine acceptance rate when HCPs use assertive/presumptive framing; drops to ~60% with suggestive/participatory approach
  • Brand-agnostic recommendations occur in 5/20 conversations — HCPs simply say "pneumonia shot" without naming a brand (opportunity for brand reinforcement)
  • Capvaxive gaining ground: when both PCV20 and PCV21 are discussed, Capvaxive is accepted in 3/7 conversations, with HCPs citing higher serotype count and superior efficacy data
  • Key drop-off risks: pharmacy referral (4/20 patients referred to pharmacy rather than in-office administration), 5-year re-dosing misconception (3/5 brand-agnostic HCPs incorrectly state the vaccine needs repeating every 5 years)

2.3 Penbraya (MenABCWY, Meningococcal Pentavalent Vaccine)

Attribute Detail
Approval October 2023 (ages 10-25)
Mechanism Combines components of Trumenba (MenB) + Nimenrix (MenACWY) into a single pentavalent vaccine
Dosing Two-dose series (0, 6 months) — replaces what was previously 4-5 separate injections across two vaccine series
ACIP Vote Recommended as an option for MenACWY + MenB vaccination in adolescents
Key value proposition Simplification — reduces total injections from 4-5 to 2, potentially improving completion rates for MenB, which has historically low uptake (~30% among 17-year-olds)

Market context:

  • GSK's MenACWY-CRM/MenB-4C pentavalent (Bexsero + Menveo combined) is a direct competitor — also received ACIP recommendations
  • The key commercial battle is which pentavalent gets preferential formulary positioning and pediatrician adoption
  • Low baseline MenB coverage creates a growth opportunity: only ~30% of 17-year-olds have received any MenB vaccine, vs. ~90% for MenACWY

3. Competitive Landscape

3.1 RSV Vaccines (Adult)

Product Company Mechanism Status Key Differentiator
Abrysvo Pfizer Bivalent prefusion F subunit Approved (adults 60+, maternal, adults 18-59 at risk) Only maternal RSV vaccine; dual-indication positioning
Arexvy GSK Adjuvanted RSV prefusion F subunit Approved (adults 60+); seeking 18+ expansion First-to-market in adults; highest sales to date (~$785M in 2025); adjuvant (AS01E) drives stronger immune response but also Guillain-Barre risk signal
mResvia Moderna mRNA-based RSV vaccine Approved (adults 60+; 18-59 at risk) mRNA platform; no Guillain-Barre signal vs. protein-based vaccines; but extremely low uptake ($8M in 2025 sales)

Competitive dynamics:

  • GSK's Arexvy holds the largest adult RSV market share but saw US sales decline ~40% in 2025 after the narrowed CDC recommendation
  • Abrysvo reached ~$1B in 2025 sales, benefiting from label expansion to 18+ at-risk and international maternal launch
  • mResvia has struggled to gain traction despite the safety differentiation (no GBS signal) — pharmacist and HCP familiarity with established brands is a major barrier
  • The CDC's narrowing of the adult recommendation (from 60+ shared decision to 75+ universal / 50-74 at increased risk) has dampened the entire adult RSV vaccine market
  • From ZoomRx HCP-Pt conversations on RSV (Abrysvo OA study, n=10): only 2/10 HCPs made a brand-specific recommendation; most described RSV vaccine generically. Disease severity was under-communicated (7/10 described RSV with low severity or not at all)

3.2 Pneumococcal Vaccines (Adult)

Product Company Valency Status Key Differentiator
Prevnar 20 (PCV20) Pfizer 20-valent Approved Market leader; "one and done" single-dose; strongest brand recognition among HCPs
Capvaxive (PCV21) Merck 21-valent Approved June 2024 Designed specifically for adults; 84% vs. 52% IPD coverage in 50+; 8 unique serotypes
Vaxneuvance (PCV15) Merck 15-valent Approved Requires follow-up with PPSV23; increasingly becoming a legacy product as single-dose options dominate
VAX-31 Vaxcyte 31-valent Phase 3 (adults); Phase 2 (infants) Potential best-in-class; >95% IPD coverage; FDA Breakthrough Therapy; expected to disrupt market post-2027
Pneumovax 23 (PPSV23) Merck 23-valent polysaccharide Legacy Polysaccharide (weaker immune response); now largely used as follow-up to PCV15

Key competitive dynamics:

  • Prevnar franchise remains the market leader (~$6B+ annually), but Capvaxive is gaining share, particularly among HCPs who discuss serotype-specific data
  • The "serotype counting" framing (21 > 20 = better) is an emerging threat to Prevnar 20 — ZoomRx data shows HCPs who compare serotype counts shift patient preference toward Capvaxive
  • Prevnar 20's competitive advantage is the serotype 4 coverage (highly prevalent in the US) that Capvaxive lacks, but this nuance is rarely communicated in clinical conversations
  • Vaxcyte's VAX-31 (31-valent, Phase 3, FDA Breakthrough) is the long-term disruptor — could capture significant share from both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive post-2027
  • The ACIP's October 2024 expansion to 50+ creates a growth tailwind for all PCVs, with both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive positioned to benefit

3.3 Meningococcal Vaccines

Product Company Coverage Status
Penbraya Pfizer MenABCWY (pentavalent) Approved (10-25 years)
MenACWY + MenB-4C (pentavalent) GSK MenABCWY Approved
Bexsero GSK MenB Approved
Trumenba Pfizer MenB Approved (component of Penbraya)
Menveo GSK MenACWY Approved
Menactra Sanofi MenACWY Approved

4. ACIP Dynamics and Market Access

4.1 Key ACIP Decisions Affecting the Portfolio

October 2024 — Pneumococcal expansion to 50+:

  • Single dose PCV (including Prevnar 20 or Capvaxive) now recommended for all adults 50+ who are PCV-naive
  • This is the most significant market expansion event for the adult pneumococcal franchise in a decade
  • Creates a "new 50" messaging anchor that HCPs are actively using in patient conversations (documented in ZoomRx HCP-Pt data)

June 2024 — RSV adult recommendation narrowed:

  • ACIP moved from "shared clinical decision for adults 60+" to "universal for 75+, shared decision for 50-74 with increased risk"
  • This narrowing has dampened the US RSV adult vaccine market for all three manufacturers
  • HCPs report confusion about the updated recommendations; in ZoomRx conversations, age thresholds cited ranged from 60 to 75 depending on the physician

October 2023 — Penbraya recommendation:

  • ACIP recommended Penbraya as an option alongside GSK's pentavalent for adolescents requiring both MenACWY and MenB vaccination
  • The commercial battle is over formulary access and physician habit formation

4.2 Market Access Challenges

Pharmacy channel importance:

  • For adult vaccines (Prevnar 20, Abrysvo), the pharmacy channel is critical — a significant proportion of vaccinations occur at retail pharmacies (CVS, Walgreens) rather than physician offices
  • ZoomRx HCP-Pt data shows 4/20 patients referred to pharmacy for pneumococcal vaccination, creating a "drop-off" risk — patients who leave the physician's office without receiving the vaccine may not follow through at the pharmacy
  • In-office vaccine stock is a major driver of same-day acceptance: 75% of patients who accepted the vaccine did so because it was available for immediate administration

Insurance and reimbursement:

  • Medicare Part D covers adult vaccines (Prevnar 20, Abrysvo) at no cost to the patient under the Inflation Reduction Act
  • Commercial insurance coverage is generally favorable post-ACIP recommendation
  • Some HCPs are uncertain about coverage, particularly for the newly expanded 50-64 age group for pneumococcal vaccines

4.3 Seasonal Patterns

  • RSV (Abrysvo): Peak vaccination window August-January; revenue heavily concentrated in Q3 and Q4
  • Pneumococcal (Prevnar 20): Less seasonal, but annual wellness visits and flu season (September-March) drive a disproportionate share of pneumococcal conversations
  • Meningococcal (Penbraya): Back-to-school season (July-September) and college enrollment deadlines create demand peaks

5. ZoomRx Research Program

5.1 Active/Completed Studies

Study Product Methodology Status Key Deliverables
Pneumococcal HCP-Patient Conversations Prevnar 20 Audio recordings of clinical conversations (n=20) Completed (Dec 2025 - Jan 2026) Listening session, full report v1, final report v2
Abrysvo OA HCP-Patient Conversations Abrysvo (adults) Audio recordings (n=10) Completed (Nov-Dec 2024) Full report with audio snippets, behavioral insights
Prevnar Adult HCP-Patient Conversations Prevnar 20 Audio recordings targeting 50-64 age group Completed (Oct-Dec 2025) Web portal, bi-weekly updates, final report
RSV PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) Abrysvo Quantitative tracking Multiple waves Rep effectiveness, message recall
RSV NPT (Non-Personal Promotional Tracking) Abrysvo Non-personal channel tracking Multiple waves Digital effectiveness

5.2 Key Research Findings Across Studies

Pneumococcal (Prevnar 20):

  1. "One and done" is the single most powerful acceptance driver — more effective than clinical efficacy data
  2. Assertive/presumptive HCP communication style ("You are due for this today") drives near-100% acceptance; suggestive style ("Would you like to get it?") yields ~60% acceptance
  3. Serotype counting anchor is emerging: when HCPs compare 20 vs. 21, Capvaxive is perceived as superior
  4. Disease severity is under-communicated — most HCPs position pneumococcal pneumonia as a manageable risk rather than life-threatening IPD
  5. Pharmacy referral is a major drop-off point; in-office stock drives same-day vaccination
  6. 5-year re-dosing misconception persists among some HCPs, undermining the "once in a lifetime" value proposition

RSV (Abrysvo adults):

  1. Brand-agnostic recommendations dominate (8/10 conversations) — HCPs rarely name a specific RSV vaccine
  2. RSV disease severity is under-communicated — most HCPs describe it as cold-like rather than potentially severe
  3. Efficacy data is NOT discussed with patients; instead, HCPs focus on dosing, side effects, and CDC recommendations
  4. "One and done" messaging resonates for RSV just as it does for pneumococcal
  5. Access and stocking matter: HCPs who stock a vaccine tend to recommend it but without naming the brand
  6. Patients do not ask about vaccine efficacy — their questions center on side effects, administration, and insurance

6. Strategic Implications for ZoomRx

6.1 Whitespace for Research

  1. Capvaxive competitive tracking: No ZoomRx study currently tracks how Capvaxive is being positioned vs. Prevnar 20 in the field. A PET-style study would quantify the competitive threat.
  2. RSV brand differentiation: With brand-agnostic recommendations dominating, Pfizer needs to understand what it would take to convert generic RSV vaccine mentions into Abrysvo-specific ones
  3. Pharmacist channel study: Given the importance of pharmacy for adult vaccination, a pharmacist-focused HCP-Pt conversation study could reveal the pharmacy-specific dynamics
  4. 50-64 age group deep dive: The ACIP expansion creates a new patient segment that requires different messaging than 65+ — understanding how HCPs introduce vaccination to "younger" adults is a high-value research question

6.2 Account Relationship Context

Pfizer Vaccines is an active ZoomRx client with an established MSA and preferred vendor status. The relationship spans multiple vaccine products (Abrysvo, Prevnar, Penbraya) with both qualitative (HCP-Pt conversations) and quantitative (PET, NPT) research streams. Vinoth oversees this engagement through East cluster operations.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

1. Disease Landscapes
1.1 Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) — Abrysvo
RSV is a common respiratory virus that typically causes mild, cold-like symptoms in healthy adults but can lead to severe lower respiratory tract disease (LRTD), hospitalization, and death in high-risk populations:

Older adults (60+): RSV causes ~60,000-120,000 hospitalizations and 6,000-10,000 deaths annually in the US among adults 65+. Those with COPD, CHF, diabetes, or immunocompromised states face the highest risk
Maternal/infant setting: RSV is the leading cause of infant hospitalization in the US (~58,000-80,000 hospitalizations/year in children <5). Seasonal peaks occur October-March in most of the continental US
Seasonality: Critical market dynamic — RSV vaccines are seasonal products, with the vaccination window running roughly August-January for the upcoming RSV season

Key market segmentation:

Older adults (75+): Universal CDC recommendation since June 2024
Adults 50-74 with risk factors: Shared clinical decision-making recommended
Maternal immunization: Abrysvo is the only RSV vaccine approved for pregnant women (32-36 weeks gestation, September-January)

1.2 Pneumococcal Disease — Prevnar 20
Streptococcus pneumoniae causes invasive pneumococcal disease (IPD) including bacteremia, meningitis, and pneumococcal pneumonia. It remains a leading cause of vaccine-preventable death in the US:

Adults 50+: The primary market for adult pneumococcal conjugate vaccines. In October 2024, ACIP expanded the recommendation from 65+ to include all adults 50+ who are PCV-naive — this is the pivotal market expansion event for Prevnar 20
Adults 19-49 with risk conditions: Risk-based recommendation unchanged (diabetes, chronic heart/lung/liver disease, smoking, alcoholism, immunocompromise)
Pediatric: Prevnar 20 is also approved for children, though the pediatric market has been long-established with Prevnar 13

Adolescents (11-25 years): Primary target population. Before Penbraya, vaccination against all five serogroups required two separate vaccine series — MenACWY (routine at 11-12, booster at 16) and MenB (shared clinical decision, 16-23)
Epidemiology: ~350 meningococcal cases annually in the US; serogroup B accounts for ~35-40% of adolescent/young adult cases

For adult vaccines (Prevnar 20, Abrysvo), the pharmacy channel is critical — a significant proportion of vaccinations occur at retail pharmacies (CVS, Walgreens) rather than physician offices
ZoomRx HCP-Pt data shows 4/20 patients referred to pharmacy for pneumococcal vaccination, creating a "drop-off" risk — patients who leave the physician's office without receiving the vaccine may not follow through at the pharmacy
In-office vaccine stock is a major driver of same-day acceptance: 75% of patients who accepted the vaccine did so because it was available for immediate administration

Insurance and reimbursement:

Medicare Part D covers adult vaccines (Prevnar 20, Abrysvo) at no cost to the patient under the Inflation Reduction Act
Commercial insurance coverage is generally favorable post-ACIP recommendation
Some HCPs are uncertain about coverage, particularly for the newly expanded 50-64 age group for pneumococcal vaccines

4.3 Seasonal Patterns

RSV (Abrysvo): Peak vaccination window August-January; revenue heavily concentrated in Q3 and Q4
Pneumococcal (Prevnar 20): Less seasonal, but annual wellness visits and flu season (September-March) drive a disproportionate share of pneumococcal conversations
Meningococcal (Penbraya): Back-to-school season (July-September) and college enrollment deadlines create demand peaks

5.2 Key Research Findings Across Studies
Pneumococcal (Prevnar 20):

"One and done" is the single most powerful acceptance driver — more effective than clinical efficacy data
Assertive/presumptive HCP communication style ("You are due for this today") drives near-100% acceptance; suggestive style ("Would you like to get it?") yields ~60% acceptance
Serotype counting anchor is emerging: when HCPs compare 20 vs. 21, Capvaxive is perceived as superior
Disease severity is under-communicated — most HCPs position pneumococcal pneumonia as a manageable risk rather than life-threatening IPD
Pharmacy referral is a major drop-off point; in-office stock drives same-day vaccination
5-year re-dosing misconception persists among some HCPs, undermining the "once in a lifetime" value proposition

RSV (Abrysvo adults):

Brand-agnostic recommendations dominate (8/10 conversations) — HCPs rarely name a specific RSV vaccine
RSV disease severity is under-communicated — most HCPs describe it as cold-like rather than potentially severe
Efficacy data is NOT discussed with patients; instead, HCPs focus on dosing, side effects, and CDC recommendations
"One and done" messaging resonates for RSV just as it does for pneumococcal
Access and stocking matter: HCPs who stock a vaccine tend to recommend it but without naming the brand
Patients do not ask about vaccine efficacy — their questions center on side effects, administration, and insurance


6. Strategic Implications for ZoomRx
6.1 Whitespace for Research

Capvaxive competitive tracking: No ZoomRx study currently tracks how Capvaxive is being positioned vs. Prevnar 20 in the field. A PET-style study would quantify the competitive threat.
RSV brand differentiation: With brand-agnostic recommendations dominating, Pfizer needs to understand what it would take to convert generic RSV vaccine mentions into Abrysvo-specific ones
Pharmacist channel study: Given the importance of pharmacy for adult vaccination, a pharmacist-focused HCP-Pt conversation study could reveal the pharmacy-specific dynamics
50-64 age group deep dive: The ACIP expansion creates a new patient segment that requires different messaging than 65+ — understanding how HCPs introduce vaccination to "younger" adults is a high-value research question

6.2 Account Relationship Context
